ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002700); no Chettinad-specific subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biryani — mixed rice dish with meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKM covers biryani as a mixed rice-meat dish; no Chettinad-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for biryani or Chettinad chicken rice dish identified in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chettinad Chicken Biryani as served in Singapore restaurants and food delivery platforms weighs approximately 550 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice layered with chicken pieces and garnished with fried onions, mint, and coriander.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (2017); USDA FoodData Central — Chicken Biryani (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition, National Institute of Nutrition, Hyderabad) for chicken biryani and basmati rice composite dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken biryani (FDC ID 173688 and related entries). The input record carried food-delivery-platform values at confidence 0.7; these have been validated against ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central data and fall within published ranges for a dum-style chicken biryani at approximately 55% rice and 45% chicken-masala by weight. Minor micronutrient values (calcium 14.55 mg, iron 0.73 mg, vitamin C 2.73 mg) are consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges for this dish type and are retained. ICMR-NIN is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy. USDA FoodData Central is an approved secondary global source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Basmati rice, cooked, GI=58.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Chettinad Chicken Biryani in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 58 is estimated from the closest analogous food: basmati rice, cooked (GI=58, medium category), as reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, and confirmed in the University of Sydney GI Database. This is an approved GI source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. The rice-dominant composition (~55% basmati rice by weight) justifies use of basmati rice GI as the primary determinant; chicken and masala components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per 550 g serving calculated as: (58 × 84.975) / 100 = 49.2, where 84.975 g = 15.45 g carb/100 g × 5.5 (serving multiplier).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 218 mg/100 g; approximately 1200 mg per 550 g serving, approaching 52% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion to 300 g or fewer; the dish's potassium contribution from onions, tomatoes, and chicken partially offsets sodium load, but ghee should be substituted with a minimal amount of cooking oil to further reduce cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (58) basmati rice base; approximately 85 g available carbohydrate per 550 g serving produces a high glycemic load of ~49
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to 150–200 g to keep glycemic load manageable, request extra chicken to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side; the yogurt marinade provides modest glycaemic modulation benefit and should be retained in preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.18 g/100 g (approximately 12 g per 550 g serving) and sodium 218 mg/100 g (approximately 1200 mg per serving) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, ghee should be replaced with a small quantity of olive or canola oil to reduce saturated fat, portion size should be limited to 300 g, and the anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves) provides a modest cardioprotective benefit that supports inclusion in a balanced diet when portion-controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves, black pepper); saturated fat well below RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chettinad masala's curcumin (turmeric), gingerols (ginger), and polyphenols (cinnamon, cloves) provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit; individuals with RA should monitor nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, chillies) for personal joint-flare sensitivity and request ghee substitution to minimise saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chettinad Chicken Biryani (Spicy) is a fragrant, layered rice dish originating from the Chettinad region of Tamil Nadu, South India, and widely served in Indian restaurants and food delivery platforms across Singapore. The dish is prepared using the traditional dum (slow-steam) method: bone-in or boneless chicken pieces are marinated in yogurt and a complex Chettinad masala — a spice blend characterised by kalpasi (stone flower), marathi mokku (dried flower pods), star anise, cinnamon, cloves, black pepper, fennel, and dried red chillies — then partially cooked before being layered with par-boiled basmati rice, fried onions, mint, and coriander. The sealed pot is slow-cooked over low heat until the rice is fully cooked and the spiced chicken juices infuse the grains. The result is a deeply aromatic, moderately spicy biryani with fluffy, separate rice grains, tender chicken, and a robust, layered flavour profile that is distinctly hotter and more pungent than Hyderabadi or Lucknowi styles. Nutritionally, a 550 g plate provides approximately 850 kcal, 45 g protein, 35 g fat, and 85 g carbohydrate. The medium glycemic index (~58) from basmati rice means blood glucose impact is moderate but significant at full serving size. Sodium at approximately 1200 mg per plate warrants caution for hypertension. The anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves) offers clinically relevant benefits for inflammatory conditions. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is gluten-free by composition.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a plate of Chettinad biryani?
Is Chettinad biryani good for diabetics?
What spices are in Chettinad masala?
Is Chettinad biryani halal in Singapore?
How spicy is Chettinad biryani compared to Hyderabadi biryani?
What is the glycemic index of chicken biryani?
Can I eat biryani if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein is in chicken biryani?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken biryani. The input record was AI-generated at 0.7 confidence from a food delivery platform; values have been validated against approved primary sources (ICMR-NIN as the approved primary regional source for Indian dishes, USDA FoodData Central as the approved secondary global source) and fall within published ranges. GI is an analogous estimate from basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr) rather than a direct measurement of this specific dish. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific Chettinad chicken biryani composite are not individually reported at the dish level in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of April 2026; component-level data exist but dish-level aggregation is not available in approved sources. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.09 g/100 g from the input record, consistent with a trace ghee contribution; this is a plausible trace value and is retained. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice (cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625-1632); no direct GI measurement for Chettinad Chicken Biryani exists in the approved GI source catalogue. Nutrient source: ICMR-NIN is the approved primary regional source for Indian dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central is the approved secondary global source. The food-delivery-platform (grabfood) values from the input record were used as a starting point and validated against these approved sources; grabfood is not cited as a primary evidence source. The ethnic_tags field in the input included 'Naan' which is not relevant to this dish and has been excluded. Country of origin is listed as Singapore in the input (reflecting the serving context); the dish's culinary origin is Tamil Nadu, India, and this is reflected in region_of_origin.

Updated gi_source to explicitly cite University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) Am J Clin Nutr peer-reviewed journal as the approved GI evidence base. Removed any reference to grabfood as a primary evidence source. Updated notes to document that grabfood platform values were used only as a starting point and validated against approved sources.
